description: Trigger discrimination for live client delivery (this skill) vs the internal server-to-server event backbone (streaming-event-architect — THE seam), request/response API and outbound webhooks (api-event-architect), the client offline write-sync engine (offline-first-sync-architect — the in-batch online-vs-offline seam), and notification UX (notification-webhook-ux-designer).

cases.prompt: Clients should get live updates over WebSocket when a shared resource changes — with per-user authorization at subscribe time, cross-node fan-out, and reconnect that replays what they missed.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — the audience is CLIENTS, the concerns are subscribe-time authz, fan-out to connections, and reconnect/replay. These are client-push mechanics, not an internal pipeline.
cases.reason: Live delivery to client connections with per-subscribe authorization is this skill. It may consume the internal backbone but does not design it.

cases.prompt: Design the internal event backbone between our services: which flows are streams vs queues, partition keys, consumer groups, dead-letter policy, retention, and CDC from the operational database.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary, reverse) — every consumer is an internal service; topics, partitions, DLQ, and CDC are backbone mechanics with no client connection in sight.
cases.reason: Server-to-server event flow is streaming-event-architect. The realtime skill explicitly stops at the boundary where it consumes that backbone to push to clients.

cases.prompt: Partners want to receive our events on their own servers: a versioned, signed webhook envelope, tenant-scoped subscriptions, at-least-once delivery with retries, and replay protection.
cases.reason: Server-to-server webhook delivery to partner endpoints is the external contract (api-event-architect). Realtime subscriptions push to a live client connection, not to a partner's HTTP endpoint.

cases.prompt: Our app must work offline: queue writes made while disconnected, apply them optimistically, and reconcile with conflict resolution when the device comes back online.
cases.note: IN-BATCH SEAM — offline write queue, optimistic apply, and conflict resolution are the sync engine. Realtime is live push while ONLINE; the two compose (the channel can be the sync transport) but the ask here is the offline engine.
cases.reason: Write-while-offline, optimistic rollback, and conflict resolution are offline-first-sync-architect. realtime-subscription-architect owns live online delivery, not the offline queue.

cases.prompt: Design the in-app notification experience: the bell/inbox, read/unread state, grouping and digesting, toast timing, and per-category preferences.
cases.reason: The presentation and UX of notifications is notification-webhook-ux-designer. Realtime owns the transport that may deliver them, not the inbox, read-state, or toast behavior.
